cataloging_subject.label: Science of Cirurgie
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged intellectual unit is one anonymous late-fourteenth-century Middle English translation of Lanfranc of Milan's Latin Chirurgia magna, organized in five treatises. Fleischhacker's 1894 edition prints the near-complete Ashmole MS 1396 text and, through Treatise II chapter IV, a second witness in Additional MS 12056 as parallel representation streams; those streams are not two separate works. The 1894 editorial frame, Latin and English supplied passages, physical witnesses, digital scans, U-M encoding, and repository XML are separate transmission layers described in the linked lineage manifest.

catalog_summary.title: Science of Cirurgie
catalog_summary.author: Lanfranc of Milan as author of the underlying Latin Chirurgia magna; anonymous Middle English translator
catalog_summary.date: Late fourteenth-century Middle English translation; near-complete witness in Bodleian MS Ashmole 1396, late fourteenth century
catalog_summary.region: England; exact place of translation unresolved; surviving witnesses exhibit Central and West Midlands language

notes: The established Middle English title appears in Fleischhacker's edition and modern scholarship; it is not attributed to Lanfranc's Latin work as an original title.

titles.value: Lanfrank's ‘Science of Cirurgie’: Edited from the Bodleian Ashmole MS. 1396 and the British Museum Additional MS. 12,056
notes: Catalog punctuation and capitalization are normalized from the 1894 title page.

agents.name: Lanfranc of Milan

notes: This source-author role does not assign the anonymous English translation or Fleischhacker's editorial interventions to Lanfranc.

A witness's scribal dialect does not prove the translation's place of origin.

language_statements.label: Latin

scope.description: Latin technical vocabulary, glosses, and short quotations embedded in the Middle English translation

notes: The underlying Latin Chirurgia magna remains a distinct expression. Fleischhacker's editorial Latin supply on pp. 258-261 is excluded from this work-language assertion and is modeled only in lineage.

form_statements.description: The medical work and its five treatises are prose; no intellectual verse lines or verse groups occur in the encoding.

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English prose translation of Lanfranc of Milan's Chirurgia magna. Its five treatises move from surgical principles, anatomy, and general wounds to wounds organized by body part, diseases and pathological conditions, fractures and dislocations, and an antidotary of medicinal preparations. The translation is substantially faithful yet adaptive and uses Middle English alongside Latin technical terminology.

The near-complete Ashmole stream represents all five treatises and a final chapter table. The Additional 12056 stream begins with a complete contents list but its surviving main text stops in Treatise II, chapter IV. Fleischhacker prints the streams in parallel through p. 157, then continues Ashmole alone through p. 360.

The two witness streams are representations of one translation, not separate works. The statement ‘The Ashmole MS. breaks off here’ on printed p. 4 closes only Ashmole's preliminary contents list; Ashmole's main text continues through all five treatises. Fleischhacker's edition is composite at selected points because it restores Ashmole readings from Additional 12056; other edition-layer interventions are documented in the lineage manifest.

extent.description: One translated surgical work in five intellectual treatises. XML structure comprises two top-level witness representations, 10 DIV2, 39 DIV3, 363 P, 515 PB, and 661 folio milestones. These structural counts do not turn the two witness streams into two works and do not count editorial chapter divisions as independent items.

content_parts.label: Science of Cirurgie
content_parts.summary: The anonymous Middle English translation as a single five-treatise surgical work, represented near-completely by Ashmole MS 1396 and fragmentarily by Additional MS 12056.
notes: Ashmole is near-complete rather than textually perfect: current scholarship identifies omission of the Latin prologue and sections III.iii.9, IV.i.1, and IV.ii.1.

content_parts.label: Treatise I: general surgery, anatomy, and wounds
content_parts.summary: Defines surgery and the surgeon's conduct, surveys anatomy, and treats wounds, bleeding, infection, and related complications.

content_parts.label: Treatise II: wounds by body part
content_parts.summary: Treats anatomy and wounds from the head downward through the face, neck, trunk, limbs, and feet.

content_parts.label: Treatise III: diseases and pathological conditions
content_parts.summary: Addresses diseases and surgical conditions organized across body regions and pathological types.

content_parts.label: Treatise IV: fractures and dislocations
content_parts.summary: Treats broken bones, dislocations, reduction, stabilization, and related care.

content_parts.label: Treatise V: antidotary
content_parts.summary: Collects medicinal preparations, compound remedies, and practical recipes used in surgical treatment.

content_parts.label: Ashmole MS 1396 representation
content_parts.summary: The near-complete witness-specific stream used as Fleischhacker's principal text, including an initial contents list, five treatises, epilogue, and final chapter table.
notes: The trailer ‘The Ashmole MS. breaks off here’ follows the preliminary contents list on p. 4 and does not mark the end of the Ashmole main text.

content_parts.label: Additional MS 12056 representation
content_parts.summary: A second witness-specific stream with a complete five-treatise contents list but surviving main text only through Treatise II, chapter IV.

related_works.title: Chirurgia magna
notes: Lanfranc's Latin work is the source expression; historical Latin witnesses and prints belong to lineage layers.

related_works.title: Anonymous surgical compilation completed in 1392 (Wellcome MS 564, ff. 10r-128r)
notes: The compilation reuses selected passages from the Science of Cirurgie tradition but is not a complete third witness of this work; its exact exemplar and contaminated relationship remain unresolved.

evidence.summary: Supplies the exact two-representation encoding boundary, electronic and source-edition statements, five-treatise structure, folio and printed-page milestones, witness endings, and editorial notes.

evidence.citation: Element, witness-stream, folio, page, and editorial-intervention audit of the pinned aha2727 XML against the 1894 scan.
evidence.locator: All DIV1-DIV4, P, PB, MILESTONE, HEAD, NOTE1, L, and LG elements and relevant attributes
evidence.summary: Counts two DIV1 witness representations, 10 DIV2, 39 DIV3, 363 P, 515 PB, 661 folio milestones, and no L or LG. It establishes the five intellectual treatise ranges, the Add 12056 stopping point, the continuing Ashmole main text after its contents-list trailer, and the edition's selected supplied passages.

evidence.citation: Lanfranc of Milan, Lanfrank's ‘Science of Cirurgie,’ ed. Robert von Fleischhacker, EETS Original Series 102 (London, 1894), complete Wellcome Collection scan.
evidence.locator: title page; correction sheet; printed pp. 1-360; especially pp. 4, 7, 157-158, 258-261, 359-360
evidence.summary: Verifies the 1894 edition's title, EETS series, complete 360-page text, parallel witness layout through p. 157, Ashmole continuation, bracketed supplies, correction leaf, and Latin insertion sourced from the 1546 Venice print and Additional MS 26106.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, AHA2727.
evidence.summary: Confirms the current electronic item title, public structured-text access, contents, and present No Copyright-United States provider statement.

evidence.citation: Folger Shakespeare Library catalog record 20711, Lanfrank's Science of cirurgie.
evidence.locator: uniform title, title, publication, extent, series, and notes
evidence.summary: Confirms the uniform title Chirurgia magna. English (Middle English), 1894 publication, 360-page extent, EETS Original Series 102, and ‘No more published.’

evidence.citation: Bibliothèque nationale de France authority record for Lanfranc de Milan.
evidence.summary: Supports Lanfranc of Milan's identity as a thirteenth- to early-fourteenth-century physician and surgeon associated with Milan and France.

evidence.citation: Marialuisa Caparrini, ‘I rimedi medici nella versione in inglese medio della Chirurgia magna di Lanfranco da Milano: alcune considerazioni preliminari sui casi di bilinguismo latino-volgare,’ Medioevo Europeo 6.2 (2022), 21-44.
evidence.locator: pp. 21-44, especially witness description, translation date and character, five-treatise outline, omissions, and bilingual terminology
evidence.summary: Current specialist study supports the anonymous late-fourteenth-century translation, Ashmole and Additional witness boundaries, near-complete Ashmole omissions, five-treatise organization, translator competence, adaptive fidelity, and Middle English-Latin bilingual terminology.

evidence.citation: Sonia Colafrancesco, ‘Il manoscritto Wellcome 564: un testimone “trascurato” della traduzione medio inglese della Chirurgia magna di Lanfranco da Milano,’ Annali Online di Ferrara – Lettere 20 (2025), 327-359.
evidence.locator: pp. 327-359, especially witness comparison, dialects, 1392 compilation, and stemmatic discussion
evidence.summary: Supports treating Ashmole and Additional 12056 as two principal witnesses of one translation, distinguishes their Central and West Midlands language, and identifies Wellcome MS 564 as an indirect selected and possibly contaminated reuse rather than a complete direct third witness.

evidence.citation: aha2727 lineage evidence for Bodleian Library MS Ashmole 1396.
evidence.summary: Supports the witness identity, fourteenth-century dating, English production, Middle English Lanfranc content, extent in Fleischhacker, and lack of a verified public complete facsimile.

evidence.citation: aha2727 lineage evidence for British Library Additional MS 12056.
evidence.summary: Supports the witness identity, fifteenth-century description, imperfect ending, physical access route, and absence of public digitized content in the current catalog.

open_questions.question: Who translated the Chirurgia magna into Middle English, when within the late fourteenth century, and where?
next_steps: Consult current linguistic and textual scholarship beyond the two open studies used here.
Keep scribal dialect evidence for Ashmole and Additional 12056 separate from claims about the translator's origin.

open_questions.question: Can Ashmole MS 1396 be dated more narrowly than the current catalog's fourteenth century, and is a complete public digital facsimile available?
next_steps: Request current codicological and reproduction information from the Bodleian, quoting MS Ashmole 1396 and both catalog ARKs.
Do not promote Fleischhacker's approximate 1380 date to a current catalog fact without newer evidence.

open_questions.question: Was Additional MS 12056 originally a complete copy of the translation, and can a public or requestable facsimile clarify its lost ending?
next_steps: Request reproduction or conservation information from the British Library for ff. 31r-86v.
Treat the complete contents list as evidence of planned structure, not proof that the surviving copy was once complete.

open_questions.question: What exemplar or contaminated branch supplied the Science of Cirurgie excerpts embedded in the 1392 surgical compilation copied in Wellcome MS 564?
next_steps: Extend Colafrancesco's passage-level comparison across every identified excerpt.
Do not model Wellcome MS 564 as copied directly from Ashmole 1396 or Additional 12056 without new evidence.

open_questions.question: Does U-M's current No Copyright-United States statement supersede the restrictive conditional availability wording embedded in the 2006 XML?
next_steps: Ask U-M Digital Collections for written clarification before redistributing the U-M-derived transcription or markup.
Keep the medieval text, 1894 edition, modern page images, and U-M encoding rights layers separate.

notes: The intellectual boundary is one translated work with five sections; parallel witness columns, missing leaves, and editorial supplies are representation and transmission facts.
Ashmole's preliminary contents list stops after Treatise II chapter X, but the main Ashmole text continues through all five treatises and a final table.
Wellcome MS 564 preserves selected adapted excerpts within a different 1392 compilation and is kept as an indirect related source, not silently promoted to a direct complete witness.

The five-treatise intellectual structure and both witness representations are modeled completely at the chosen work-and-treatise granularity; the 39 encoded chapter-level divisions are summarized rather than repeated as separate content parts.
Fleischhacker's correction sheet and other source-supply interventions belong to edition and lineage layers, not to the intrinsic intellectual-content hierarchy.
